THE
INDIA LIST
AND
INDIA OFFICE LIST
FOR
1902.

COMPILED FROM OFFICIAL RECORDS BY DIRECTION OF THE SECRETARY
OF STATE FOR INDIA IN COUNCIL.

An oval-shaped library stamp with the words 'INDIA OFFICE' arched across the top and 'LIBRARY' arched across the bottom. There are two small dots on the left and right sides of the oval.

LONDON:
HARRISON AND SONS, 59, PALL MALL,
BOOKSELLERS TO HIS MAJESTY AND H.R.H. THE PRINCE OF WALES.

1902.

Price Ten Shillings and Sixpence.

PREFACE.

IN the lists of Officers serving in India, which are contained in pages 7 to 101 of this volume, only substantive appointments are shown ; and, except in the case of members of the Indian Civil Service, the names of Officers drawing substantive pay of less than Rs. 500 a month are not included.

In the Record of Services (pages 379 to 592) a different principle of selection has been followed, as explained on page 377.

The matter hitherto contained under the headings "Regulations for Admission to the Indian Services" and "Selections from the Indian Military, Medical and Marine Regulations" has been re-arranged, and Tables of Contents have been prefixed which, it is hoped, will facilitate reference (see pages 179, 283, and 317). Some important additions have also been made (see pages 200, 201, 215, 217, 218), including a Table of Sterling Equivalents of Rupee Salaries (page 218), and Regulations regarding the Standard of Vision required of candidates (page 215).

A notice of the newly formed North West Frontier Province will be found on page 70.

A general Alphabetical Honours List appears on pages 151 to 168. Recipients of the decoration of the Royal Red Cross are for the first time included.

A combined Casualty List is given on pages 593 to 597.

It is requested that intimations of errors in the lists, or of omissions, may be addressed to the Editor of the India List, India Office, S.W.

INDIA OFFICE AND GOVERNMENT OF INDIA.

Messrs. P. S. KING & SON have been appointed by the Secretary of State for India, Agents for the Sale of the Publications of the Government of India and of the India Office. They are prepared to supply copies of the Reports and publications printed by the Government Printer in Calcutta and of the Departmental Reports, etc., on sale at the various Government Printing Presses in India. Such works as are not stocked in this country will be obtained from India. Lists of these publications appear regularly in their monthly catalogues. A Catalogue of the more important of these publications, post free.

INDIA. New Catalogue of Parliamentary Reports relating to India, from 1800-1900. Post free on application.

THE INDIA LIST AND OFFICE LIST.

OFFICE OF

THE SECRETARY OF STATE FOR INDIA

IN COUNCIL,

ST. JAMES'S PARK, S.W.

SECRETARY OF STATE.

THE RIGHT HON. LORD GEORGE HAMILTON, M.P.

UNDER SECRETARIES OF STATE.

Sir Arthur Godley, K.C.B. ; The Earl of Hardwicke.

ASSISTANT UNDER SECRETARY OF STATE.

Sir Horace G. Walpole, K.C.B., J.P.

INDIAN TROOP SERVICE.

The business of the Troop Service is under the superintendence of Rear-Admiral G. T. H. Boyes, R.N., Director of Transports at the Admiralty.

ROYAL INDIAN ENGINEERING COLLEGE, COOPER'S HILL.

BOARD OF VISITORS.

CHAIRMAN.

Sir C. H. T. Crosthwaite, K.C.S.I., Chairman, Public Works Committee of Council of India (ex officio).

MEMBERS.

Sir Alexander M. Rendel, K.C.I.E., M.Inst.C.E. (Consulting Engineer to Secretary of State for India).

Hugh Leonard, M.Inst.C.E. (late Chief Engineer, P.W.D., India).

Colonel G. F. Pearson (late Officiating Director General of Forestry, India).

Colonel Sir W. S. S. Bisset, R.E., K.C.I.E. (late Government Director of Indian Railway Companies).

Sir C. P. Ilbert, K.C.S.I. (late Member of the Council of the Governor-General of India).

Sir John Wolfe Barry, K.C.B., F.R.S., Past-President of the Institution of Civil Engineers.

Sir W. H. Preece, K.C.B., F.R.S., M.I.C.E. (late Engineer-in-Chief and Electrician, General Post Office, and Past President, Institution of Civil Engineers).

Sir E. H. Busk, M.A., LL.B.

Prof. A. R. Forsyth, D.Sc., Fellow of Trinity College, Cambridge.

G. C. Bourne, Esq., M.A., Fellow of New College, Oxford.

J. G. H. Glass, Esq., C.I.E. (late Chief Engineer, Public Works Department, India).

J. Mansergh, Esq. (Past-President of Institution of Civil Engineers).

STAFF OF THE COLLEGE.

PRESIDENT.—Col. J. W. Ottley, C.I.E., R.E.

Secretary.—Lieut.-Colonel W. J. Boyes.

Bursar.—C. Tickell, M.I.C.E.

Chaplain.—Rev. F. E. Hutchinson, M.A.

Medical Officer.—H. E. Giffard.

ENGINEERING AND APPLIED MECHANICS BRANCHES.

Professor of Engineering Construction and Surveying.—A. W. Brightmore, D.Sc., M.Inst.C.E.

Assistant Professor of Engineering.—R. J. Woods, M.E., F.C.H., M.Inst.C.E.

Instructor in Surveying.—Vacant.

Professor of Electro-Technology and Physics.—A. Hay, D.Sc., M.Inst. Electrical Engineers.

Demonstrator in Electro-Technology.—A. F. T. Atchison.

Instructor in Geometrical Drawing and in Estimating, and Lecturer in Architecture.—A. Hicks.

Assistant Instructor in Geometrical Drawing and Instructor in Freehand Drawing.—C. B. McElwee.

Lecturer in Accounts.—R. G. Macdonald.

Superintendent of the Workshops and the Mechanical Laboratory.—Hopps.

MATHEMATICAL BRANCH.

Professor of Applied Mathematics.—G. M. Minchin, M.A., F.R.S.

Professor of Pure Mathematics.—A. Lodge, M.A., late Fereday Fellow of St. John's College, Oxford.

NATURAL SCIENCE BRANCH.

Lecturer in Chemistry.—F. E. Matthews, Ph.D., F.I.C.

Analytical Chemist.—F. W. Harbord, Assoc. Royal School of Mines, F.I.C.

Lecturer in Geology and Mineralogy.—Professor H. G. Seeley, F.R.S.

Demonstrator in Physics.—W. S. Templeton, M.A., B.Sc.

FORESTRY BRANCH.

Principal Professor of Forestry.—W. Schlich, C.I.E., Ph.D., F.R.S., F.L.S., late Inspector-General of Forests to the Government of India.

Assistant Professor of Forestry.—W. R. Fisher, B.A., Conservator of Forests, Indian Forest Service.

Lecturer on Botany and Entomology.—P. Groom, D.Sc., M.A., F.L.S.

Lecturer on Organic Chemistry.—F. E. Matthews, Ph.D., F.I.C.

UNCLASSIFIED SUBJECTS.

Instructor in French.—J. A. Perret.

Instructor in German.—Dr. A. Weiss.

GOVERNMENT OF INDIA.

VICEROY AND GOVERNOR-GENERAL OF INDIA

His Excellency the Right Hon. LORD CURZON OF KEDLESTON, G.M.S.I., G.M.I.E., 6th January, 1899.

PERSONAL STAFF OF THE GOVERNOR-GENERAL.

Private Secretary, Walter Roper Lawrence, C.I.E.

Military Secretary, Lt.-Col. the Hon. E. Baring, 10th Hussars.

Aides-de-Camp, Capt. R. G. T. Baker-Carr, Rif. Brig.; Capt. C. Champion de Crespigny, 2nd Life Guards; Capt. C. Wigram, 18th Bengal Lancers; Capt. the Hon. G. B. Portman, 10th Hussars.

Extra Aides-de-Camp, Suffolk, Lieut. the Earl of, 4th Batt. Glouc. Regt.

Native Aides-de-Camp, Hukam Sing, Risaldar Major, Sirdar Bahadur, 16th Ben. Lanc., and Debi-dayal Singh, Risaldar Major, Sardar Bahadur, Gov. Genl.'s Body Guard.

Surgeon, Lieut.-Col. E. H. Fenn, C.I.E., R.A.M.C.

COUNCIL.

Ordinary Members—

Thomas Raleigh. Took his seat, 3 April, 1899.

Sir E. F. Law, K.C.M.G. Took his seat, 31 March, 1900.

Major-Gen. Sir E. R. Elles, K.C.B. Took his seat, 11 April, 1901.

A. T. Arundel, C.S.I. Took his seat, 18 May, 1901.

D. C. J. Ibbetson, C.S.I. Took his seat,

Extraordinary Member—

Gen. Sir A. P. Palmer, G.C.I.E., K.C.B., Commander-in-Chief in India. Took his seat, 22 April, 1901.

Additional Members, for the purpose of making Laws and Regulations, nominated under 24 & 25 Vic., c. 67, sec. 10, and 55 Vic., c. 14:—

Name. Date of Nomination. Province or Body represented.
Official Members.
Sir A. Wingate, K.C.I.E. .... 4 October, 1900 Bombay.
F. A. Nicholson, C.I.E. .... 30 October, 1900 Madras.
D. M. Smeaton, C.S.I. .... 6 November, 1900 Burma.
C. W. Bolton, C.S.I. .... 27 November, 1900 Bengal.
R. G. Hardy, C.S.I. .... 7 December, 1901 N.W. Provs. and Oudh.
Non-Official Members.
Rai Sri Ram Bahadur .... 14 December, 1900 N.W. Provs. and Oudh.
G. K. Gokhale .... 1 May, 1901 Bombay.
M. R. Ry. P. Ananda Charlu, Vidia Vinodha Avargal, Rai Bahadur, C.I.E. .... 18 October, 1901 Madras.
L. P. Pugh .... 16 November, 1901 Calcutta Bar.
Saiyid Husain Bilgrami .... 29 November, 1901
R. P. Ashton .... 29 November, 1901
Rai Bahadur B. K. Bose, C.I.E. .... 19 December, 1901 Central Provinces.
M. R. Rameshwara Singh, Bahadur of Darbhanga .... 19 December, 1901 Bengal.
M. C. Turner.... 7 January, 1902 Calcutta Chamber of Commerce.

GRADATION LIST OF COVENANTED CIVIL SERVANTS ON
THE BENGAL ESTABLISHMENT.

Substantive appointments only are stated. The names of all Civil Servants will also be found in the Lists relating to the Provinces and Departments in which they are serving.

BURMA.ADMINISTRATION.

Lieutenant-Governor—The Hon. Sir F. W. R. FRYER, K.C.S.I. Appointed Chief Commissioner, 3rd April, 1895; Lieutenant-Governor 1st May, 1897.

Private Secretary—Capt. F. A. B. Fryer, 6th Dragoons.

Aide-de-Camp—Lieut. J. S. S. Dunlop, 2nd Batt., South Staffordshire Regt.

Honorary Aides-de-Camp—Major H. Parkin, S.C., and Commander G. E. Holland, R.I.M., D.S.O., C.I.E.

Council of the Lieutenant-Governor for the purpose of making Laws and Regulations—

Hkun Saing, C.I.E., Sawbwa of Hsipaw,
1st Aug., 1899.

D. Norton, C.S.I., 3rd Jan., 1900.

J. Benton, 12 Nov., 1900.

J. Lowis, 12 Nov., 1900.

D. R. Cameron, 12 Nov., 1900.

H. M. S. Mathews, 3 Dec., 1900.

D. H. R. Twomey, 28 March, 1901.

J. G. Findlay, 28 March, 1901.

Chief Secretary—C. G. Bayne.

Revenue Secretary—H. M. S. Mathews.

Secretary—J. B. Wingate.

Under Secretary—G. B. H. Fell.

Assistant Secretaries—B. P. Lentaigne and W. H. L. Cabell.

Financial Commissioner—D. M. Smeaton, C.S.I.

Secretary to Financial Commissioner—W. J. Keith.

Asst. Sec. to Financial Commr.—W. F. Noyce.

Secretary, P.W.D.—H. Groves.

Secretary, P.W.D. (Railway Branch)—J. S. Brown.

Under Secretaries, P.W.D.—A. E. Rose and E. G. Stanley.

Settlement Commissioner—T. C. Wilson.

Director of Land Records and Agriculture—A. Gaitskell.

Asst. ditto—J. C. Clancey, F.R.A.S., F.R.G.S.

SURVEY AND TELEGRAPH DEPARTMENTS.

The names of the officers in the Telegraph and Survey Departments serving in the Punjab will be found under "India." (See pp. 30 and 31.)

POLITICAL DEPARTMENT.

AGENTS TO THE LIEUTENANT-GOVERNOR.

The Commissioner and Superintendent of the Delhi Division for the time being, for Pataudi, Dujana, Loharu, Kalsia, Sirmur (Nahan).
Do. of the Jullundur Division for the time being, for Kapurthala, Mandi, Suket, Faridkot and Maler Kotla.
Do. of the Lahore Division for the time being, for Chamba.
The Superintendent, Hill States, Simla, for the time being, for the Hill States.

HILL STATES.

Comprising Kahlur (Bilaspur), Bashahr, Hindur (Nālagarh), Keonthal, Baghal Baghat Jubbal, Kumharsain, Bhajji, Mailog, Balsan, Dhami, Kuthar, Kunhiar, Mangal, Bija, Darkuti, Taroch, Sāngri, Kaneti, Dalti, Koti, Theog, Madhan, Ghond, Ratesh, Rawin, and Dhadi. The Deputy Commissioner of Simla is ex officio Superintendent.

PATIALA.

Medical Adviser to the Maharaja—Lieut.-Col. C. W. Owen, C.M.G., C.I.E., I.M.S.

NORTH-WEST FRONTIER PROVINCE.

By a Proclamation of the Government of India in the Home Department, dated the 25th Oct., 1901, a new province was formed, with effect from the 9th November, under the title of the "North-West Frontier Province," and constituted a Chief Commissionership under the administration of a Chief Commissioner. The new province includes the district of Peshawar, Kohat and Hazara, the Bannu and Marwat tahsils of the district of Bannu, and the Tank, Dera Ismail Khan and Kulachi tahsils of the district of Dera Ismail Khan, the said territories having been formerly under the administration of the Lieutenant-Governor of the Punjab.

The following appointments have been made to the North-West Frontier Province :—

Agent to Gov. Gen. and Chief Commr.—Lieut.-Col. H. A. Deane, C.S.I., S.C., apptd. 9th Nov., 1901.
Judicial Commr.—C. E. F. Bunbury.
Sec. to Chief Commr.—A. H. Grant.
Asst. Sec. to Chief Commr.—R. J. R. Glancy.
Personal Asst. to Chief Commr.—Lieut. H. B. St. John, S.C.

Agency Surgeon and Administrative Medical Officer—Major W. A. Sykes, M.B., D.S.O.
Inspector Gen. of Police—C. G. W. Hastings, C.I.E.
Sessions Judges :—
Peshawar Division—Major E. Inglis.
Derajat Division—F. Field.

BOMBAY.

Governor—THE RIGHT HON. HENRY STAFFORD, BARON NORTHCOTE, G.C.I.E.

Took his seat 17th February, 1900.

PERSONAL STAFF.

Private Secretary—C. H. A. Hill.

Military Secretary—Lt.-Col. R. Owen, S.C.

Aides-de-Camp—Capt. the Hon. C. B. F. Greville, 7th Hussars; Lt. J. G. Greig, 7th Bombay Infantry.

Extra Aides-de-Camp—Capt. M. D. Wood, West Yorkshire Regiment, and Capt. C. V. Sloane-Stanley, 12th Lancers.

Native Aide-de-Camp—Risaldár Sheik Abdul Hamid, of the Governor's Body Guard.

Medical Officer—Lieut.-Colonel A. E. J. Croly, F.R.C.S.I., R.A.M.C.

Commandant of the Governor's Body Guard—Capt. J. H. Pollard, 1st Bombay Lancers.

Adjutant of the Governor's Body Guard—Capt. J. H. Crawford, 2nd Bombay Lancers.

COUNCIL.

Sir E. C. K. Ollivant, K.C.I.E., took his seat 24th April, 1897.

J. Monteath, C.S.I., took his seat 6th August, 1900.

2nd Class.

The District and Extramural Prisons are in most cases in charge of the Civil Surgeon.

Presidency Postmaster, Bombay—W. Merrett.

Railway Mail Service—A. F. Ryan.

1806 Hon. W. Fullarton Elphinstone (2nd time). Edward Parry.
1807 Edward Parry. Charles Grant (2nd time).
1808 Edward Parry (2nd time). Charles Grant (3rd time).
1809 Charles Grant (2nd time). William Astell.
1810 William Astell. Jacob Bosanquet (3rd time).
1811 Jacob Bosanquet (3rd time). Sir Hugh Inglis, Bart. (3rd time).
1812 Sir Hugh Inglis, Bart. (3rd time). Robert Thornton.
1813 Robert Thornton. Hon. W. Fullarton Elphinstone.
1814 Hon. W. Fullarton Elphinstone (3rd time). John Inglis.
1815 Charles Grant (3rd time). Thomas Reid.
1816 Thomas Reid. John Bebb.

(a.) On the death of Mr. Boulton, the Court, on October 12, appointed Edward Wheeler, Chairman, and John Harrison, Deputy.

(b.) Afterwards Sir George Wombwell, Bart.

(c.) Sir Henry Fletcher being disqualified, the Court, on November 26, appointed Nathaniel Smith, Chairman, and on the next day, William Devaynes, Deputy.

(d.) Afterwards Sir Stephen Lushington, Bart.

(e.) Mr. Scott resigned the Chair, and, on September 2, the Court appointed Chas. Mills, Chairman, and John Roberts, Deputy.

(f.) Afterwards Sir William James, Bart.

(g.) Afterwards Sir Henry Fletcher, Bart. Mr. Gregory being disqualified, the Court, on July 31, appointed Sir Henry Fletcher, Chairman, and, the next day, Nathaniel Smith, Deputy.

(h.) William Devaynes appointed, 2nd December, Deputy Chairman, in place of John Michie, deceased.

PRESIDENTS AND GOVERNORS OF FORT ST. GEORGE IN MADRAS.
Names. Assumed charge of office. Remarks.
William Gyfford (a) ... .. 16 June, 1684
Elihu Yale (b) ... .. 25 July, 1687
Nathaniel Higginson ... .. 3 Oct., 1692
Thomas Pitt ... .. 7 July, 1698
Gulston Addison ... .. 18 Sept., 1709 Died at Madras, 17 Oct., 1709.
Edmund Montague ... .. 17 Oct., 1709 Acting governor.
William Fraser ... .. 3 Nov., 1709 Acting governor.
Edward Harrison ... .. 11 July, 1711
Joseph Collet ... .. 8 Jan., 1717
Francis Hastings ... .. 18 Jan., 1720 Acting governor.
Nathaniel Elwick ... .. 15 Oct., 1721
James Macrae ... .. 15 Jan., 1725
George Morton Pitt ... .. 14 May, 1730
Richard Benyon ... .. 23 Jan., 1735
Nicholas Morse (c) ... .. 17 Jan., 1744
John Hinde (d) ... .. ...
Charles Floyer (e) ... .. 16 April, 1747
Thomas Saunders (f) ... .. 19 Sept., 1750
George Pigot ... .. 14 Jan., 1755
Robert Palk ... .. 14 Nov., 1763
Charles Bouchier ... .. 25 Jan., 1767
Josias DuPre ... .. 31 Jan., 1770
Alexander Wynch ... .. 2 Feb., 1773
Lord Pigot (g) ... .. 11 Dec., 1775
George Stratton ... .. 23 Aug., 1776 Suspended from the service.
John Whitehill ... .. 31 Aug., 1777 Acting governor.
Sir Thomas Rumbold, Bart. ... .. 8 Feb., 1778
John Whitehill (h) ... .. 6 April, 1780
Charles Smith ... .. 8 Nov., 1780 Acting governor.
Lord Macartney, K.B. ... .. 22 June, 1781

(a) Madras, which had previously been an Agency under the jurisdiction of the Presidency of Bantam in Java, was created a Presidency in 1652, Bantam being in turn placed under Madras, and the President, Mr. Baker, transferred to the latter station. This arrangement, however, continued only to 1655, when the settlement again became an Agency, and so remained till 1684, when the Agent, Mr. Gyfford, was made "President and Governor of all the English East India Company's affairs upon the coast of Choromandel and in the Bay of Bengala."

(b) Mr. Yale also acted during Mr. Gyfford's absence in Bengal from 8th Aug., 1684, to 26th Jan., 1685.

(c) Madras having been captured by the French on the 10th September, 1746, the government of the Settlement devolved on Mr. John Hinde, the Deputy Governor of Fort St. David.

(d) Mr. Hinde died at Fort St. David on the 14th April, 1747, previous to the receipt of the Court of Directors' despatch of 24th Jan., 1747, creating Fort St. David the Head Settlement, and appointing Mr. Hinde president and governor.

(e) The Court's despatch ordering Mr. Floyer's dismissal from the service was received at Fort St. David on the 6th July, 1750.

(f) The seat of government was re-established at Madras on the 5th April, 1752, four years after its restoration to the English by the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle.

(g) Governor for the second time. By order of Mr. George Stratton and the majority of the Council he was placed under arrest and detained at St. Thomas's Mount on the 24th Aug., 1776. He was allowed to return to the Madras Garden House on the 28th April for change of air, and died there on the 10th May, 1777.

(h) Acting governor second time. Suspended by the Governor-General and Council under Section IX of the Regulating Act.

(a) Acted as Viceroy and Governor-General of India from the 2nd Dec., 1863, to 12th Jan., 1864.

(b) Embarked for Calcutta on the 19th Feb., 1872, for the purpose of assuming temporarily the office of Viceroy and Governor-General of India on the Earl of Mayo's death.

GOVERNORS OF BOMBAY.
Names. Assumed charge of office. Remarks.
Sir Abraham Shipman (a) ... .. Appointed 'General and Governor' on 19 March, 1662, but was prevented from landing in Bombay by the Portuguese, and died on the island of Anjediva in October, 1664.
Humfrey Cooke ... .. Secretary to Sir Abraham Shipman, succeeded him in command, and came to Bombay as governor in February, 1665. He remained in power till 5 November, 1666.
Sir Gervase Lucas ... .. 5 Nov., 1666 Died 21 May, 1667.
Captain Henry Garey ... .. 22 May, 1667 Officiating.
Sir George Oxenden ... .. 23 Sept., 1668 Died in Surat, 14 July, 1669.
Gerald Aungier ... .. 14 July, 1669 Died in Surat, 30 June, 1677.
Thomas Rolt ... .. 30 June, 1677
Sir John Child, Bart. (b) ... .. 27 Oct., 1681
Bartholomew Harris ... .. 4 Feb., 1690 Died in Surat, 10 May, 1694.
Daniel Annesley ... .. 10 May, 1694 Officiating.

(a) The first four governors held Bombay for the Crown. The Island was handed over to the Company on the 23rd Sept., 1668. For the next nineteen years (1668–1687), except for occasional visits, and during three years (1672–1675) of Governor Aungier's rule, the Governors of Bombay spent almost the whole of their time in Surat, of which factory they were presidents. During this time, Bombay was administered by an officer styled Deputy-Governor. The transfer, in 1687, of the headquarters of the Company's power to Bombay to a great extent did away with the need of a Deputy-Governor. But in spite of the change the title continued for many years to be borne by the second member of the Council. It would seem to have fallen into disuse some time between 1720 and 1738.

(b) Child was Governor and General, with his headquarters in Bombay, whither he moved from Surat on the 2nd May, 1687, and where he died on the 4th Feb., 1690. In the year 1683, Bombay was the scene of a revolt against the Company's authority. The head of the rebellion was Captain Richard Keigwin, the third member of the Council. Placing the Deputy-Governor under arrest, Keigwin ruled Bombay in the King's name from the 27th Dec., 1683, to the 19th Nov., 1684, when on promise of pardon he handed over the Island to Admiral Sir Thomas Grantham.

Names. Assumed charge of office. Remarks.

Lestock Robert Reid ... .. 6 Aug., 1846 Officiating.

(a) Under Gayer, Waite, and Aislabie—that is, from 1694 to 1715—Bombay Governors held the title of General. During the last three years (1701-1704) of his nominal command, Gayer was in confinement in Surat.

(b) Proceeded to Madras on duty in Aug., 1793, and thence joined the Council of the Governor-General as Commander-in-Chief in India on the 28th Oct., 1793.

(c) Was appointed Governor of Bombay by the Honourable the Court of Directors, on the 4th Aug., 1841, but, before he could take charge of his appointment, he was assassinated in Cabul on the 23rd Dec., 1841.

NOTE.—Mr. J. F. D. Inglis made over charge on the 15th February, 1877, from which date the offices of Chief Commissioner of Oudh and Lieutenant-Governor of the North-Western Provinces were amalgamated under the order of the Government of India, Home Department, No. 45, dated 17th January, 1877 (Gazette of India, 20th January, 1877, Part I).

THE MOST EXALTED ORDER OF THE STAR OF INDIA.

INSTITUTED 1861. ENLARGED 1866, 1875, 1876 AND 1897.

SOVEREIGN OF THE ORDER.

THE KING.

GRAND MASTER OF THE ORDER.

The VICEROY and GOVERNOR-GENERAL OF INDIA for the time being

(His Excellency LORD CURZON OF KEDLESTON, G.M.I.E.).

KNIGHTS GRAND COMMANDERS (G.C.S.I.).

IMPERIAL ORDER OF THE CROWN OF INDIA.

INSTITUTED 1ST JANUARY, 1878.

SOVEREIGN OF THE ORDER.

THE KING, EMPEROR OF INDIA.

LADIES OF THE ORDER (C.I.).

(With the Dates of their Appointments.)

Her Majesty The Queen, 1 Jan., 1878.

WARRANT OF PRECEDENCE IN INDIA.

VICTORIA, by the Grace of God of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland Queen, Defender of the Faith, Empress of India,—

To all to whom these presents shall come :

WHEREAS it hath been represented unto Us that it is advisable that the rank and precedence of persons holding appointments in the East Indies as regulated by Our Royal Warrant, dated the 18th day of October, 1876, should be altered, We do therefore hereby declare that it is Our will and pleasure that in lieu of the table laid down in Our said recited Warrant, the following table be henceforth observed with respect to the rank and precedence of the persons hereinafter named, viz. :—

  1. 1. Governor-General and Viceroy of India.
  2. 2. Governors of Madras and Bombay.
  3. 3. President of the Council of the Governor-General.
  4. 4. Lieutenant-Governor when in his own territories.
  5. 5. Commander-in-Chief in India.
  6. 6. Lieutenant-Governor.
  7. 7. Chief Justice of Bengal.
  8. 8. Bishop of Calcutta, Metropolitan of India.
  9. 9. Ordinary Members of the Council of the Governor-General.
  10. 10. Commander-in-Chief of Her Majesty's Naval Forces in the East Indies.
  11. 11. Chief Justice of a High Court other than that of Bengal.
  12. 12. Bishops of Madras and Bombay.
  13. 13. Ordinary Members of Council in Madras and Bombay.
  14. 14. Lieutenant-Generals Commanding the Forces, Punjab, Bengal, Madras, and Bombay.
  15. 15. Chief Commissioners of the Central Provinces and Assam, Residents at Hyderabad and in Mysore, and Agents to the Governor-General in Rajputana, Central India, and Baluchistan.
  16. 16. Puisne Judges of a High Court.
  17. 17. Chief Judge of a Chief Court.
  18. 18. Military Officers above the rank of Major-General.
  19. 19. Additional Members of the Council of the Governor-General for making Laws and Regulations.
  20. 20. Bishops of Lahore, Rangoon and Lucknow.
  21. 21. Secretaries to the Government of India.
  22. 22. Commissioner in Sind.
  23. 23. Judges of a Chief Court, Recorder of Rangoon and Judicial Commissioners, Burma.
  24. 24. Chief Secretaries to the Governments of Madras and Bombay.
  25. 25. Major-Generals, Members of a Board of Revenue, Commissioners of Revenues and Customs, Bombay, and Financial Commissioners, Punjab and Burma.

26. Judicial Commissioners, including Additional Judicial Commissioners of Oudh, the Central Provinces, and Sind.

27. Additional Members of the Councils of the Governors of Madras and Bombay for making Laws and Regulations, Members of the Legislative Council of a Lieutenant-Governor.

28. Vice-Chancellors of Indian Universities.

FIRST CLASS.

29. Members of the Indian Civil Service of 30 years' standing.

30. Advocate-General, Calcutta.

31. Comptroller and Auditor-General.

32. Commissioners of Divisions, the Superintendent of Port Blair, and Residents, Political Agents, and Superintendents drawing Rs. 2,000 a month and upwards (not being Collectors or Deputy Commissioners of British Districts), within their respective charges.

33. Chief Secretaries to Local Governments other than those of Madras and Bombay.

34. Surveyor-General of India, Directors-General of the Post Office, of Telegraphs in India, and of Railways, Chief Engineers, first class, Accountants-General, Military and Public Works Departments, Director, Royal Indian Marine, and Manager, North-Western Railway.

35. Bishops (not territorial) under license from the Crown.

36. Archdeacons of Calcutta, Madras and Bombay.

37. Brigadiers-General.

38. Commissioners of Divisions.

39. Commissioner of Northern India Salt Revenue, and Opium Agents, Benares and Bihar.

40. Secretaries and Joint Secretaries to Local Governments, and Private Secretary to the Viceroy.

SECOND CLASS.

41. Members of the Indian Civil Service of 23 years' standing and Colonels.

42. Military Secretary to the Viceroy.

43. Judicial Commissioners of the Hyderabad Assigned Districts and Baluchistan; the Superintendent of Port Blair; Residents, Political Agents, and Superintendents drawing Rs. 2,000 a month and upwards (not being Collectors or Deputy Commissioners of British Districts).

44. Inspector-General of Forests in India, and Director of the Geological Survey.

45. Standing Counsel to the Government of India.

46. Directors of Public Instruction, and Inspectors-General of Police and Prisons under Local Governments, and Accountants-General.

47. Survey Commissioner and Director of Land Records and Agriculture, Bombay Commissioners of Settlements; and Controllers of Military Accounts.

48. Chief or Senior Civil Secretary to a Local Administration.

49. Chief Engineers, second and third classes; Deputy Surveyor-General; Deputy Director-General of Telegraphs in India, and Director-in-Chief, Indo-European Telegraph Department.

50. Divisional, and District and Sessions Judges, Collectors and Magistrates of Districts; Deputy Commissioners of Districts; Deputy Superintendent of Port Blair; and the Chief Officer of each Presidency Municipality, within their respective charges.

51. Archdeacons of Lahore, Lucknow, and Rangoon.

52. Deputy Secretaries to the Government of India.

53. The Senior Chaplains of the Church of Scotland in Bengal, Madras, and Bombay.

54. Remembrancers of Legal Affairs and Government Advocates under Local Governments.

55. Officers in the First Class Graded List of Civil Offices not reserved for Members of the Indian Civil Service.

THIRD CLASS.

56. Members of the Indian Civil Service of 18 years' standing and Lieutenant-Colonels.

57. The Deputy Director, Royal Indian Marine.

58. The Assistant Director, Royal Indian Marine.

59. Commanders and Inspectors of Machinery, Royal Indian Marine.

60. Political Agents and Superintendents drawing less than Rs. 2,000 a month (not being Collectors or Deputy Commissioners of British Districts) within their own charges.

61. Secretaries to Local Administrations other than those already specified.

62. Consulting Engineers to the Government of India for Railways.

63. Private Secretaries to Governors.

64. Military Secretaries to Governors.

65. Administrators-General.

66. Sanitary Commissioners under Local Governments; Postmasters-General; the Comptroller, Post Office; and Conservators of Forests, first grade.

67. Directors of Public Instruction and Inspectors-General of Police and Prisons under Local Administrations and Comptrollers and Deputy Auditors-General.

68. Managers of State Railways other than the North-Western Railway; Chairman of the Port Trust, Bombay; and Chairman of the Port Trust, Calcutta.

69. Vice-Chairman of the Port Trust, Calcutta; Directors of Traffic and Construction, Indian Telegraph Department; Examiners of Accounts, Public Works Department, first class; Officers of the Superior Revenue Establishment of State Railways, first class, first grade; Superintending Engineers, Public Works Department, first class; Superintendents of the Survey of India Department, first grade.

70. Inspectors-General of Registration and Directors of Land Records and Agriculture, under Local Governments.

71. Senior Chaplains other than those already specified.

72. Sheriffs within their own charges.

73. Officers in the Second Class Graded List of Civil Offices not reserved for Members of the Indian Civil Service.

FOURTH CLASS.

74. Members of the Indian Civil Service of 12 years' standing, and Majors.

75. Lieutenants of over 8 years' standing, and Chief Engineers of the Royal Indian Marine.

76. Government Solicitors.

77. Inspectors-General of Registration, Sanitary Commissioners, and Directors of Land Records and Agriculture under Local Administrations.

78. Officers in the Third Class Graded List of Civil Offices not reserved for Members of the Indian Civil Service.

The entries in the above table apply exclusively to the persons entered therein, and, while regulating their relative precedence with each other, do not apply to the non-official community resident in India, the members of which shall take their place according to usage.

Officers in the above table will take precedence in order of the numbers of the entries. Those included in one number will take precedence inter se according to the date of entry into that number.

When an officer holds more than one position in the table, he will be entitled to the highest position accorded to him.

Officers who are temporarily officiating in any number in the table will rank in that number below permanent incumbents.

All officers not mentioned in the above table, whose rank is regulated by comparison with rank in the army, to have the same rank with reference to civil servants as is enjoyed by Military Officers of equal grades.

All other persons who may not be mentioned in this table, to take rank according to general usage, which is to be explained and determined by the Governor-General in Council in case any question shall arise.

Nothing in the foregoing Rules to disturb the existing practice relating to precedence at Native Courts, or on occasions of intercourse with Natives, and the Governor-General in Council to be empowered to make rules for such occasions in case any dispute shall arise.

All ladies to take place according to the rank herein assigned to their respective husbands, with the exception of wives of Peers, and of ladies having precedence in England independently of their husbands, and who are not in rank below the daughters of Barons, such ladies to take place according to their several ranks, with reference to such precedence in England, immediately after the wives of Members of the Council of the Governor-General.

Given at Our Court at Windsor this tenth day of December, in the year of Our Lord One thousand eight hundred and ninety-eight, and in the sixty-second year of Our Reign.

By Her Majesty's Command.

(Signed) GEORGE HAMILTON.

TABLE OF SALUTES IN INDIA.
Persons. No. of Guns.
Imperial Salute ... .. 101 From saluting stations within Indian territories and seas,* and on being fallen in with by or visiting any of His Majesty's ships within those limits.
The King and Emperor when present in person } 31
Members of the Royal Family ... .. 31
Royal Standard and Royal Salute ... .. 31
Viceroy and Governor-General in India ... .. 21
Independent Asiatic Sovereigns ... .. 21
Other Foreign Sovereigns ... .. 21
Members of their Families and their Standards... .. 19
Ambassadors ... .. 17
Governors of Presidencies ... .. 17
The President of the Council of India ... .. 17 From saluting stations within Indian territories and seas, and by His Majesty's ships within those limits.
Governor-General of Portuguese Settlements in India 17
Governor of Pondicherry ... .. 17
Governors of His Majesty's Colonies ... .. 17
Lieutenant-Governors of Provinces in India ... .. 15 According to military rank, with two guns added.
Field-Marshals or Admirals of the Fleet ... .. 17
Commander-in-Chief in India ... .. ...
Commander-in-Chief of His Majesty's Naval Forces ... According to naval rank, with two guns added.
Provisional Commander-in-Chief ... .. ... According to military rank, with two guns added.
Generals and Admirals, or their Flags .. .. 15 With two guns added within the limits of their own commands.
Members of Council ... .. 15
Plenipotentiaries and Envoys ... .. 15
Lieut.-Governors of His Majesty's Colonies ... .. 15
Lieut.-Generals commanding the forces, Punjab, Bengal, Madras, and Bombay 13
Vice-Admirals, Lieut.-Generals, or their Flags... .. 13
Agents to the Viceroy and Governor-General ... .. 13
Residents ... .. 13
Chief Commissioners of Provinces, and Commissioner of Sind 11
Rear-Admirals & Major-Generals, or their Flags 11
Political Agents and Chargés d'Affaires ... .. 9 At any place where there is a saluting station, within the Indian territories and seas, and by His Majesty's ships within those limits.
Commodores of the first-class, and Brigadier-Generals 9
Consuls-General ... .. 9
The Portuguese Governor of Damaun ... .. 9
The Governor of Diu ... .. 9 Gun for gun, but only when such ships are saluting the national flag on arrival.
Return salutes to Foreign Men-of-war ... .. ...
Return salutes to Captains of the Navy, and Naval Officers of inferior rank † 7

* Indian territories are understood to include all the waters of India within three miles of the coast thereof. Indian seas to extend from the North-West entrance of the Straits of Malacca to Cape Comorin, excepting Ceylon, and, from Cape Comorin to Aden, including the Maldivic and Laccadive Islands, and the Persian Gulf.

† Personal salutes are not returned.

TABLES OF SALARIES SANCTIONED FOR THE CHIEF OFFICERS* OF THE ADMINISTRATION OF INDIA.

N.B.—Exchange Compensation, referred to below, is an allowance granted to secure certain classes of Indian public servants against serious depreciation of their emoluments by the fall in the sterling value of rupee salaries. It is not granted to some high officers (including the Governor-General, Governors, and Lieutenant-Governors), nor does it in any case exceed a fixed maximum amount. The rate, which takes the form of a percentage on salary, is liable to variation; but when the exchange value of the rupee is 16 pence, as at present, the percentage on salary is 6\frac{1}{3}, and the maximum amount Rs. 138,14.3 per mensem.

The following tables (which must not be held to be authoritative and are liable to variation) show the approximate equivalent in sterling, when the exchange value of the rupee is 16 pence, of the annual salaries in rupees of the undermentioned officers, together with exchange compensation so far as it is allowed.

compensation allowance

PARTICULARS REGARDING ADMISSION TO THE INDIAN SERVICES.
