(b) Provided that any annuitant, who is a permanent resident in India, may, if he wishes to draw his annuity in that country, exercise the option of receiving it at the rate of exchange annually fixed for the adjustment of transactions between the British and Indian Exchequers.

Note.—[This rule applies to all Officers whose pensions are stated in sterling, and who, being resident in Asia, take payment in India.]

Transfer of Payment.

1073. Transfer from the Home Treasury to an Indian treasury, and vice versa, is permitted twice only.

Payment in a Colony.

1076. Payment of annuities may be made in any of the Colonies named in Appendix No 17, in accordance with the procedure laid down in Chapter XLIV., Articles 1051 to 1059.

or Bombay—from the Accountant-General at Bombay or Madras (as the case may be); showing his service, residence, the amount of pension to which he is entitled, and the date from which his pension may be paid.

1079. Applications in India.—A Chaplain belonging to the Bengal Ecclesiastical Establishment, who wishes to obtain pension from the authorities in India, must submit his application to the Government of India in the Home Department, through the Archdeacon or Bishop of his Diocese.

1080. A Chaplain belonging to the Madras or Bombay Ecclesiastical Establishment must submit his application to the Government of Madras or the Government of Bombay, as the case may be, through the Archdeacon or the Bishop of his diocese.

1081. A Chaplain of the Church of Scotland serving in the Bengal Presidency, who wishes to obtain pension from the authorities in India, must submit his application to the Government of India in the Home Department, if he is not Senior Chaplain, through the Presidency Senior Chaplain of the Church of Scotland.

1082. A Chaplain of the Church of Scotland serving in Madras or Bombay must submit his application to the Local Government, if he is

PART X.—PROCEDURE RELATING TO PENSIONS—continued.

not the Presidency Senior Chaplain, through the Presidency Senior Chaplain.

1083. The Home Department, before accepting the resignation of a Chaplain on the Bengal Establishment, or a Chaplain of the Church of Scotland serving in the Bengal Presidency, should obtain a certificate from the Comptroller, Indian Treasuries, showing the Chaplain's service, residence, and the amount of pension to which he is entitled. The Government of Madras or Bombay, as the case may be, should obtain a like certificate from the Accountant-General of the Presidency concerned.

1084. Grant of Pension in India.—(a) In the case of the Bengal Presidency, if the Chaplain's

resignation is accepted, the case should be forwarded to the Finance Department of the Government of India, in which department a resolution will be recorded granting the pension to which the Chaplain may be entitled.

(b) In the case of the Madras or Bombay Presidency, the procedure is the same, but the resolution is issued by Madras or Bombay, as the case may be.

1085. Last-pay Certificate.—A Chaplain to whom pension has been granted in India, should be careful before proceeding to England to obtain the usual certificate of the last issue of pay or pension to him in India.

PART XI.—CHAPTERS XLVII. TO LIII. contain Regulations relating to TRAVELLING ALLOWANCES.

EXTRACT FROM CHAPTER I.—OFFICERS APPOINTED IN EUROPE.

1195. The rules regarding—

(a) Passage to India, and outfit allowances (in case of appointment in Europe),
(b) Return to Europe (on termination of appointment),
of certain high officers, which have been framed by His Majesty's Secretary of State for India, are given in Appendix 29.

1196. Officers appointed in Europe to the Telegraph, Public Works, Forest, and Educational Departments, to the Geological Survey of India, and to the Bengal Pilot Service, are ordinarily allowed by the Secretary of State a free passage to India.

1197. Chaplains also are ordinarily allowed a free passage to India, and in the contracts now entered into by them they engage to refund the cost of their passage to India, in the event of their resigning their appointments within three years after the date of their arrival in India on grounds other than ill-health, under a medical certificate.

1197A. If the health of a Chaplain appointed on probation compels him to leave India during the probationary period, he must resign the service entirely, in which case he will not be entitled to anything beyond a free return passage to his country.

A probationer removed from the service for misconduct of any kind, or who resigns his

position for any cause other than certified ill-health, will not be entitled to a return passage to his country, and will be bound to refund the cost of his passage to India.

1198. An Officer appointed to the Bengal Pilot Service is granted an Outfit Allowance of £20.

1200. An Officer, who is appointed by the Secretary of State, while resident in Europe, and who is not one of the High Officers mentioned in Article 1195, is entitled to Travelling Allowance at the rates laid down in Chapter XLVIII. from the capital town of the Presidency to which he is attached, to the first station to which he is posted.

1201. (a) If such an Officer disembarks in India at any port other than the capital town of the Presidency to which he is attached, he is entitled to Travelling Allowance from such port to the first station to which he is posted, limited to the amount to which he would have been entitled under the preceding Article if he had disembarked at such capital town.

(b) But if an Officer is directed by the Secretary of State to proceed to a particular port, he is entitled to Travelling Allowance from that port.

1202. For the purposes of the preceding Articles, an Officer attached to any Province or Presidency, other than Bombay or Madras, is held to be attached to the Bengal Presidency.

PART XII.—APPENDICES.APPENDIX No. 17.Referred to in Articles 936 and 1052.LIST OF COLONIAL TREASURERS, DISTRICT PAYMASTERS, AND OTHER IMPERIAL AGENTS IN THE COLONIES.
Station. Designation of Agent.
Bahamas The Registrar of Deeds, Nassau, Bahamas.
Barbados (including British Guiana, Trinidad, &c.) The District Paymaster, Barbados.
Bermuda The District Paymaster, Bermuda.
British Columbia Included in the Dominion of Canada (see below).
British Honduras The Colonial Treasurer, Belize, British Honduras.
Dominion of Canada and Newfoundland The District Paymaster, Halifax, Nova Scotia.
Cape of Good Hope The District Paymaster, Cape Town.
Ceylon The District Paymaster, Ceylon.
Cyprus The District Paymaster, Cyprus.
Falkland Islands The Colonial Treasurer, Falkland Islands.
Fiji Islands The Governor, Fiji Islands.
Gambia The District Paymaster, Sierra Leone, West Africa.
Gibraltar The District Paymaster, Gibraltar.
Gold Coast The Colonial Treasurer, Accra, West Africa.
Hong Kong The District Paymaster, Hong Kong.
Jamaica The District Paymaster, Jamaica.
Lagos The District Paymaster, Sierra Leone, West Africa.
Malta The District Paymaster, Malta.
Mauritius The District Paymaster, Mauritius.
Natal The District Paymaster, Pietermaritzburg, Natal.
New South Wales The Colonial Treasurer, Sydney, New South Wales.
New Zealand The Colonial Treasurer, Wellington, New Zealand.
Nova Scotia Included in the Dominion of Canada (see above).
Queensland The Colonial Treasurer, Brisbane, Queensland.
Sierra Leone The District Paymaster, Sierra Leone, West Africa.
South Australia The Colonial Treasurer, Adelaide, South Australia.
St. Helena The Acting District Paymaster, St. Helena.
Straits Settlements The District Paymaster, Straits Settlements.
Tasmania The Colonial Treasurer, Hobart, Tasmania.
Transvaal The District Paymaster, Pietermaritzburg, Natal.
Vancouver's Island Included in the Dominion of Canada (see above).
Victoria The Colonial Treasurer, Melbourne, Victoria.
Western Australia The Colonial Treasurer, Perth, Western Australia.
APPENDIX No. 29.Referred to in Article 1195.RULES REGARDING THE ALLOWANCES FOR EQUIPMENT AND VOYAGE OF CERTAIN OFFICERS.

RULES made under the Statute 43 Vic., cap. 3, by the Secretary of State for India in Council, fixing the Allowances for Equipment and Voyage of the Governor-General of India, Members of Council of the Governor-General of India, Governors of Madras and Bombay, Commanders-in-Chief of the Forces in India, Bishops of Calcutta, Madras, and Bombay. Dated 3rd of January, 1888.

I.

1. The allowance for equipment and voyage of a Governor-General of India, resident in Europe at the time of his appointment, is fixed at £3,500.

2. Except as declared in these Rules, Government will make no arrangements for, and will defray none of the expenses connected with, the conveyance or passage of a Governor-General, his family, or suite, to or from India.

3. A newly appointed Governor-General proceeding to India from Europe to enter upon his office may be accompanied by three combatant military officers for his personal staff, the expenses of whose passage will be defrayed by Government according to the rules in force in the Military Department as to military officers proceeding to India on duty.

4. For an ex-Governor-General returning to Europe on resignation, a steamer belonging to the Indian Marine Service will (if consistent with the requirements of the public service) be gratuitously provided, to convey him and his family and suite to Suez; but the homeward voyage of the ship thus provided shall not be continued beyond Suez. The ex-Governor-General will bear the cost of entertainment and messing incurred while on board, in respect of himself and his party, according to the rules in force relating to the conveyance of passengers in Indian Marine troopships.

5. No grant from Indian revenues will be made to any ex-Governor-General for his homeward journey from Suez.

6. An ex-Governor-General returning to Europe may be accompanied by two officers of his personal staff, for whom free passages to England will be allowed according to the rules in the Military Department, and also free return passages to India, if they start on their return voyage within two months from the date of quitting India.

7. In the event of a Governor-General dying, and leaving a widow in India, a steamer belonging to the Indian Marine Service will (if consistent with the requirements of the public service) be provided to convey her to Suez in like manner as in the case of an ex-Governor-General. The widow of a deceased Governor-General so returning to Europe may be accompanied by one officer of the personal staff on the conditions mentioned in the preceding rule, and shall bear all such expenses as would, in ordinary course, be borne by an ex-Governor-General returning to Europe.

II.

8. The allowances for equipment and voyage of the Governors of Madras and Bombay, and the Commander-in-Chief of the Forces in India, if resident in Europe at the time of appointment respectively, are fixed as follows:—

The Governor of Madras... ..1,000
The Governor of Bombay... ..1,000
The Commander-in-Chief of the Forces in India...... ..500

9. A Commander-in-Chief of the Forces in India proceeding from Europe to assume his command may be accompanied by three combatant military officers for his personal staff, and newly-appointed Governors of Madras and of Bombay, similarly proceeding from Europe to take up their duties, may respectively be accompanied by two combatant military officers for their personal staff, the passages of these officers being provided at the expense of Government according to the rules in force in the Military Department as to military officers proceeding to India on duty; but with these exceptions, Government will make no arrangements for, and will defray none of the expenses connected with, the passage or conveyance of any of the officers mentioned in Rule 8, or of their families, to or from India.

10. An ex-Governor of Madras or Bombay, or an ex-Commander-in-Chief of the Forces in India, or the widow of a Governor or of a Commander-in-Chief who may die in India leaving a widow there, returning to Europe, may be accompanied by one officer of his personal staff, for whom a free passage will be allowed by Government according to the rules in force in the Military Department, and also a free return passage if he starts on the return voyage within two months from the date of quitting India.

III.

11. The allowances for equipment and voyage of Members of the Council of the Governor-General of India, and of Bishops of Calcutta, of Madras, and of Bombay, if resident in Europe at the time of appointment respectively, are fixed as follows:—

Members of the Council of the Governor-General of India... ..£
300
The Bishop of Calcutta... ..300
The Bishop of Madras... ..300
The Bishop of Bombay... ..300
RULES REGARDING THE ALLOWANCES FOR EQUIPMENT AND VOYAGE OF CERTAIN OFFICERS.—continued.

12. An officer of any of the Indian Services, temporarily residing in Europe, or a military officer transferred from one superior command in India to another during temporary residence out of India, shall not be deemed to be resident in Europe at the time of appointment, within the meaning of Rule 8 or Rule 11.

IV.

RULES made under Statute 24 & 25 Vic., cap. 104, sec. 6, by the Secretary of State in Council of India, consolidating and amending the rules fixing (when necessary) the Expenses for Equipment and Voyage of the Chief Justices and Judges of the several High Courts established under the said Statute. Dated 31st of January, 1888.

39. For the purpose of defraying the expenses of equipment and voyage from Europe on first appointment, there shall be allowed,—

To a Chief Justice or Judge of any High Court ... .. £300

But no such allowance shall be made to any person, who, being in India, is appointed to the office of Chief Justice or Judge, or who, having been in India, is in Europe at the time of his appointment with the intention of returning to India.

APPENDIX No. 29B.

Referred to in the Note to Article 607.

Special Pensions to Barristers.

Special pensions are admissible as follows, but the Officers named in Article 604 are otherwise subject to the 'Ordinary Pension' regulations in Part IV:—

  1. (i) After not less than six years and nine months' active service in one or other of the offices specified in Article 604,—an Invalid pension of £300 a year.
  2. (ii) After not less than eight years and eight months' such service,—an Invalid pension of £500 a year.
  3. (iii) After not less than eleven years and six months' such service,—a Retiring pension of £750 a year.

The Active Service of the Officers referred to in Article 604 includes, besides time spent on duty, whether in substantive or acting service, Privilege Leave, Subsidiary Leave, and periods of vacation during which the Officer is not on Furlough or Extraordinary Leave.

SELECTIONS FROM THE INDIAN MILITARY REGULATIONS.

The following regulations are published for information, but it should be understood that they are only selections. Complete copies of the Indian Army Regulations can be seen on personal application to the Military Department, India Office.

INDIAN STAFF CORPS.

Examination for Regimental and Army Staff Appointments.*

Officers of the Staff Corps are not qualified to hold the appointments of Commandant,† Second in Command, or Squadron or Double Company Commander, nor to leave their regiments for the purpose of holding any appointment whatever, except that of Aide-de-camp (unless they come within the provisions of the following paragraphs), until they shall have passed an examination in the following subjects:—

PART I.

(a.)—Cavalry—

In the field:—

Command of a regiment in the following circumstances:—

Singly on parade.

As part of a brigade.

As advanced or rear guard.

As outpost covering a brigade or division.

Mancœuvring on varied ground (adapting movements to the ground and to a supposed object).

(b.)—Infantry—

Equitation.

In the field:—

Command of a battalion in the following circumstances:—

Singly on parade.

As part of a brigade.

As advanced or rear guard.

As outpost covering a brigade or division.

Skirmishing (adapting movements to the ground and to a supposed object).

PART II.‡

(c.) Military law.

(d.) Duties in the field.

1. Military engineering.

2. Military topography.

3. Tactics.

(g.) Organisation and Equipment.

Officers may be nominated at any time to staff appointments for distinguished service in the field, although they may not have passed the qualifying Examination; but officers so appointed, who are liable to Examination, will be required to qualify within a reasonable time.

The Examination in Part I., with the exception of equitation, is not required from an officer who has held the permanent appointment of Adjutant of a regiment for a continuous period of not less than twelve months.

All officers in the Staff Corps who may have passed the final Examination at the Staff College will only be examined in Part I., and those officers who have passed the Examination for admission to the Staff College in Military Law, Fortification, Topography, and Tactics, will not be required to pass in Part II., provided they held the rank of Captain at the time of the Examination, or were Lieutenants of at least eight years' service. They must, however, pass a Supplementary Examination in subject (g) and the Indian Articles of War and Indian Military Law as laid down in the footnote below.

Promotion.

Under Royal Warrant promotion in the Staff Corps is governed by such Regulations as the Secretary of State for India may make from time to time.

The existing regulations are—

The promotion of Second Lieutenants from the Unattached List is governed by Rule 7 of the Regulations for Admission (page 202);

and that of Second Lieutenants from the Army by para. 3 of the Regulations for appointment under Rule 13 (page 203). Officers after 9 years' service become Captains; After 18 years' service, they become Majors; and After 26 years' service, they become Lieutenant-Colonels.

* The qualifications required and Examinations to be passed for the Judge Advocate-General's Department, the Administrative Departments of the Army, the Survey Department, and for Civil and Political Employ, will be found in the Indian Army Regulations.

† Officers will not be appointed to the command of regiments until they have passed the prescribed examination in "Tactical Fitness for Command."

‡ Officers who pass Part II. while on leave in England (see page 295) will be required to pass a Supplementary Examination in the Indian Articles of War and Indian Military Law on their return to India. Should they not have passed Part I., they must do so within six months of their return to their appointments in India, failing which they will be required to pass Part II. again.

INDIAN STAFF CORPS—continued.

Time on half pay not exceeding one year is allowed to reckon as service for promotion in the case of an officer placed on half pay on account of ill-health contracted in the performance of military duty, but no officer can be promoted while actually on the half-pay list.

A Lieutenant-Colonel becomes eligible for promotion to the substantive rank of Colonel in the Army after three years' satisfactory service with that rank. Selection for promotion will be made under such regulations as the Secretary of State in Council may lay down.

A Lieutenant-Colonel may, if selected, be promoted to the rank of Brevet-Colonel after four years' service in the substantive rank of Lieutenant-Colonel in the command of a regiment or battalion, or in any appointment which may be considered to be analogous thereto. The regulations under which a Lieutenant-Colonel may be granted the substantive rank of Colonel will apply to Brevet-Colonels also.

Officers above the rank of Lieutenant are also eligible for promotion by brevet as a reward for distinguished service in the field, or for distin-

guished service of an exceptional nature other than in the field.

Promotion to the ranks of Major-General and Lieutenant-General will only be made by selection to fill appointments, or as a reward for distinguished service. There will be an establishment of three Generals.

A Major, substantive or brevet, may be granted the temporary rank of Lieutenant-Colonel, and a Captain the temporary rank of Major, while holding the permanent appointment of Commandant of a Native regiment.

Officers in civil employ, after ten years' absence from military duty, are removed from the effective list of the army, and placed on a supernumerary list, rising thereon, under the regulations in force, to the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel, but to no higher rank.

This condition is, however, not applicable to officers of rank taken for high political or civil employment, or to officers taken for temporary civil or political duties in the field or in newly-acquired territories.

Pay.*

STAFF CORPS PAY (WHILE REQUIRED TO REMAIN IN INDIA).

For any Month. For any Month.
R. A. P. R. A. P.
General Officer 1,295 5 0 Captain 374 1 6
Colonel in the Army and Lieutenant-Colonel 827 14 0 Lieutenant 225 12 0
Major 640 14 0 Second Lieutenant

Officers holding appointments receive either a staff salary in addition to the above pay, or consolidated pay inclusive of it. The monthly rates of consolidated pay and staff salaries are at present as follows:—

Leave Pay.

Officers below the rank of Major-General on leave out of India receive leave pay at the following rates:—

Yearly. Yearly.
£ £
After appointment to the Staff Corps After the commencement of the 20th
After the commencement of the 5th year's service for pension ...
year's service for pension ... Ditto 25th ditto ...
Ditto 15th ditto ... Ditto 30th ditto ...
... ...
200 450
250 600
300 700

Half-Pay.

Unemployed General Officers will receive half-pay at the following rates:—

Yearly. Yearly.
£ £
Field Marshal Lieutenant-General
... ...
1,300 650
800 Major-General
... ...
... 500

Half-pay is only granted to other officers on account of certified unfitness for duty (see page 294).

The rates of half-pay are those in force in the British Service, namely:—

Daily. Daily. Daily.
s. d. s. d.
Lieutenant-Colonel ... 11 0 Lieutenant (after six years' commissioned service) ...
Major ... 9 6 " (after ten years' commissioned service) 4 0
Captain ... 7 0 4 6
" having higher rank by brevet ... 8 0
Lieutenant ... 3 0

An officer cannot remain more than six years on temporary half-pay.

* For the present sterling equivalents of the salaries specified see page 218.

“LEAVE RULES FOR THE INDIAN STAFF CORPS.”

(India Army Circulars, April, 1886, Clause 51, published 16th April, with subsequent decisions.)

I. An officer may be granted,—
Privilege leave under such regulations as may from time to time be in force.
Leave out of India, for no longer period than one year, capable of extension to two years' absence from duty, on the following pay:—

£

After appointment to the Staff Corps ... 200 a year.
After the commencement of the 5th year's service for pension ... 250 "
Do. do., 15th, do., do., do. 300 "
Do. do., 20th, do., do., do. 450 "
Do. do., 25th, do., do., do. 600 "
Do., do., 30th, do., do., do. 700 "

Leave in India, but for the period of one year only, on full military pay, and half the Staff salary of appointment.

II. No extension of leave involving absence from duty for more than two years, whether taken in or out of India, can be granted except on specially urgent grounds and without pay.

III. An officer unable on account of the state of his health to return to duty within the maximum period of two years' absence, will, unless he is under rule 2 specially granted an extension of leave without pay, be placed on temporary half pay or the retired list, as the circumstances of the case may seem to require. An officer will also be liable to be placed on half pay or the retired list should his health require an undue amount of leave, whether in or out of India. This power will be exercised at the instance of the Government of India should the officer be in India, or, should he be at home, by the Secretary of State in Council.

IV. All leave, in or out of India, will count as service for pension.

V. Leave, whether it is leave in India or leave out of India, will not involve forfeiture of appointment, and may be granted at any time, but solely at the discretion of the Commanders-in-Chief of the several armies for officers serving directly under their orders, and by the Governor-General in Council or the Governors in Council for officers of civil departments of the army or of Corps not under the direct orders of a Commander-in-chief.

VI. An officer on leave, whether in India or out of India, will be required to rejoin at once on being recalled to duty, unless certified by a Medical Board as unfit to do so.

VII. The above rules shall apply to—
Officers whose admission to the Staff Corps shall bear date subsequent to the 30th June, 1886.

Officers of the Staff Corps, including probationers, or of the Cavalry and Infantry lists of the Indian Army, in military employment, who may elect them within twelve months of that date.

Officers in civil employ on that date, subsequently reverting to military duty, may elect them within six months from the date of being replaced at the

disposal of the Military Department. Such officers who may be on leave when so replaced must make their election at once. Those taking leave within the six months must make their election before going on leave.

They shall not apply to—
Officers in permanent civil employ.
Officers commanding districts or holding other appointments under special regulations as to leave of absence, who continue subject to such regulations.

Note.—The above does not preclude general officers commanding districts, who before their appointment were subject to the leave rules for the Staff Corps, from being granted short leave in India under these rules.

VIII. Officers who, under Rule VII, have elected to come under these rules count all previous leave as service for pension. The position of those who have not elected them remains unchanged.

IX. The Secretary of State in Council reserves the right of making any change in the foregoing regulations which circumstances may require.

Officers who have not elected for the above rules in the manner and within the period laid down in Rule VII have no further opportunity of election, but remain under previous rules. It must also be clearly understood that no modifications of the existing codes will be sanctioned, and that there is no middle course between abiding by them and acceptance of these new rules.

Military officers in civil employ on 30th June, 1886, who may desire to retire direct from such employment, have, on such retirement, the option of electing the new rules.

When it may be necessary to convert sterling rates of pay under these rules into rupee currency, or vice versa, the conversion will be made at the rate of exchange in force for the period when the pay became due, as fixed annually for the adjustment of financial transactions between the Indian and Imperial Governments.

The rules which govern the allowances of officers acting for absentees remain unchanged.

INDIAN MEDICAL SERVICE.

The rules laid down for the grant of leave to officers of the Indian Staff Corps apply equally to officers of the Indian Medical Service in military employ, with the following modification in regard to the rates of pay whilst on leave out of India:—

After arrival in India ... £ 200 a year.
After the commencement of the 5th year's service for pension ... 250 "
Ditto, ditto, 10th ditto .. 300 "
Ditto, ditto, 15th ditto ... 400 "
Ditto, ditto, 20th ditto ... 450 "
Ditto, ditto, 25th ditto ... 500 "

The rules apply to all Medical Officers admitted to the service after the 30th June, 1886, and to officers who may elect them under the provisions of Rule VII.

SUBSIDIARY RULES.

1. Officers in permanent civil employment, while they continue in such employment, are not affected by these rules; but if desirous of retiring on pension direct from such employment, they will, whether on duty or on furlough, be given the benefit of the rule which allows all previous leave to count as service for pension.

It is, however, to be clearly understood that no officer in civil employment will be allowed to place himself out of employment for the purpose of taking leave under these rules, or for any purpose but immediate retirement.

2. Leave in or out of India commences on the date an officer is “struck off duty,” which means the date following the day of relief, and terminates on, and includes, the day preceding the date on which he rejoins his regiment or appointment on return from leave; the only exception being in the case of leave out of India on medical certificate, when the precise amount recommended by a Medical Board to be necessary in any case reckons from the date on which the vessel in which he embarks quits her moorings or anchorage, and terminates on the date preceding that on which he rejoins his regiment or appointment on return.

3. In the case of an officer obliged to leave his station to appear before a Medical Board at another station, the date of being “struck off duty” will mean the date he is permitted to quit the station where he is examined and invalidated. An officer so circumstanced is entitled to full pay and Staff salary up to and for the date preceding that on which he is permitted to quit the station where he is examined and invalidated.

4. Under these rules, absence from duty with pay, which is restricted to two years, is reckoned from the date of being “struck off duty” to date of rejoining appointment or regiment.

5. Up to date of leaving India, and from date of return to India to date of rejoining regiment or appointment, an officer granted leave out of India under these rules will receive Indian pay and half staff salary, provided, in any case, the two years' absence from duty is not exceeded.

6. An officer subject to these rules who holds a permanent departmental or regimental staff appointment, promoted while on leave out of India, is only entitled, from date of debarkation in India to that of joining his appointment, to the Indian pay and allowances of his rank, and the half staff salary of the appointment he held at the time he proceeded on leave.

7. An officer holding a permanent departmental or regimental staff appointment, promoted while on leave in India, will draw the Indian pay and allowances of his rank, and the

half staff salary of the appointment he held at the time he proceeded on leave, until he assumes charge of the duties of the appointment to which he has been promoted.

8. The Indian Pension Regulations are unaffected by these leave rules, except that all leave from date of commencement of service for pension will, under them, be allowed to count as service for pension.

9. Time passed at Addiscombe after the attainment of the age of 16 years will reckon as service for pension in calculating the rates of pay under Rule I.

10. The periods of service for pension in Rule I of the leave rules will entitle an officer taking leave to the pay fixed for such periods of service, without any limitation based on the rule* which prevents an officer from retiring on the pension fixed for a particular period of service, unless he has served half of that service in the Staff Corps.

11. The provisions of the rules with regard to duration of leave do not apply to probationers of the Staff Corps.

12. The limit of two years laid down for leave will not apply to time spent at the Staff College, which counts as duty.

13. Officers recalled from leave granted under these rules will be given free passage to India, or not, according to the Transport Regulations in force at the time.

14. Advances of pay to officers proceeding on and returning from leave out of India will be granted as heretofore.

15. Extensions of leave, except on medical certificate or in special or urgent cases in which the Secretary of State decides according to circumstances, cannot be granted without the assent of the proper authority in India as defined in Clause V of the rules.

16. Extensions of leave beyond one year under these rules are not claimable in any way as a right, but are admissible only at the discretion of the proper authority, and then on special and urgent grounds.

17. An officer subject to these rules on promotion to Major-General, or on attainment of Colonel's allowance, cannot be granted leave under them, and, if permitted to reside in England or elsewhere out of India, is restricted to the English pay of his rank.

18. Officers subject to these rules, who are in the following categories, and who entered the Indian Army or Staff Corps before the 1st July, 1881, may elect to reside out of India on the allowances admissible under them until succession to the Colonel's allowance or promotion to Major-General, whichever may happen first; or to remain in India on the unemployed Indian pay and allowances of their rank if unemployed:—

(a) Officers vacating regimental commands, remaining in the service for the attainment of the highest retirement pension, or the Colonel's allowance.

(b) Officers over 55 years of age, who may be required under the rules to vacate staff, departmental, or civil appointments, and for whom other suitable employment cannot be found.

(c) Officers of the Commissariat, Military Accounts, and Judge Advocate General's Department, who vacate their appointments on attaining to 52 years of age.

INDIAN STAFF CORPS—continued.“LEAVE RULES FOR THE STAFF CORPS”—continued.

(d) Officers who on the 8th August, 1884, were holding appointments of unlimited tenure to which they were appointed before the 1st January, 1884, and who vacate their appointments on completing seven years' tenure from date of appointment or such further extension as may be granted to enable them to complete 32 years' service for pension not exceeding nine years in all.

G. G. O. 432 of 1884, Clause VII.

(e) Squadron or wing commanders who vacate their appointments on attaining 52 years of age.

(f) Colonels who entered the Staff Corps after the 12th September, 1866, and before the 1st July, 1881, and Colonels of the General List, after transfer to the Unemployed Supernumerary List.

The limit of absence from duty imposed by the leave rules will not be held applicable to these officers.

19. Officers who may have entered civil employment after the 30th June, 1886, and before the 1st July, 1887, without having elected the leave rules for the Staff Corps, will be entitled, on reverting to military duty, to elect them under the conditions laid down in the third clause of Rule VII. (See note to next paragraph.)

20. Officers who may have entered permanent civil employment after the 30th June, 1886, having previously elected the leave rules for the Staff Corps, and officers who may have entered the Staff Corps under those rules and subsequently taken civil employment, are absolutely under the leave rules contained in the Civil Service Regulations so long as they remain in civil employment, but on reverting to military duty within ten years of their entry into civil employment, as allowed under G. G. O. No. 135 of 1884, they again come under these rules.

Note.—An officer does not come under civil rules by reason of being transferred to a civil appointment, the tenure of which is limited to a definite period, nor by officiating for less than three years in the civil department.

21. Officers struck off the effective list of the army under the provisions of G. G. O. No. 135 of 1884, after ten years' absence from military duty, cannot elect or revert to the leave rules for

the Staff Corps, except for the purpose of immediate retirement, with the advantage of counting all leave as service for pension, as provided for in paragraph 1.

22. The service for furlough of an officer, who at the time of coming under the civil leave rules, was subject to the leave rules for the Staff Corps, is calculated in accordance with the civil leave rules retrospectively from the date of his arrival in India, i.e., he is credited with an amount of furlough equivalent to one-fourth of his active service, less any leave out of India actually taken.

It is notified that all officers in military employ, irrespective of rank or the furlough or leave regulations to which they may be subject, who require an extension on private affairs of their furlough or leave out of India, must obtain the previous assent of the authorities in India under whom they may be serving, to apply for such extension.

This permission, when obtained, must be attached to their application to the India Office for the required extension.

Note.—This memorandum has been compiled for easy reference, but it is not intended for quotation as of official authority.

India Office,
August, 1893.

FURLOUGH AND LEAVE RULES OF 1875.

(WITH SUBSEQUENT ALTERATIONS.)

G.G.O. No. 1316, dated 30th December, 1875.

I. Officers included in Schedule A may receive, after five years' service in India or under the Government of India, one year's furlough, and an additional year for each subsequent five years' service. Furlough not taken at the time it becomes due is not lost, and can, subject to these rules, be taken later.

Note.—Service for furlough under these rules, in the case of an officer electing them, is reckoned from the date of his election. (See also Notes 2, 3, and 4 to Schedule A.)

II. An officer may receive at any time any portion of the furlough to which he is entitled; but, except under Rule VIII, he may not be absent from duty on any ground for more than three years

consecutively; nor, on return from furlough, may he receive, except under Rule VI, any further portion of the furlough to which he is entitled until after the interval of two years.

III. It must be distinctly understood that furlough is subject to the exigencies of the public service. Officers commanding regiments, and Heads of Departments, will forward no application for furlough unless able to satisfy the superior authorities that the officer's services can be spared without any inconvenience to the public service.

IV. Furlough does not involve forfeiture of appointment, may be spent anywhere, and counts as service for pension. (See Rule VIII.)

Note.—All furlough necessitated by wounds or illness contracted by active service in the field is treated as “extraordinary furlough” to the extent of two years, reckoning as service for pension, and as not subject to repayment, irrespective of whether it is earned by length of service or not.

V. A commissioned officer on furlough receives (up to, but not beyond, the sum of £1,000 a year) half the emoluments of his office, computed at is. 6d. to the rupee, the privileged rate of exchange at which furlough allowances are payable from the Home Treasury; but in any case he does not receive less than £250 a year. The emoluments of his office include all pay, staff corps pay, staff salary, and Indian allowances he would receive in respect to it if he were on duty, and for the purpose of each payment his office is the office he fills on the day when that payment becomes due.

2. A Departmental Officer with honorary rank or Warrant Officer receives half the emoluments of his office, calculated in the same manner as those of Commissioned Officers, no such Officer, however, receiving as furlough pay less than the English furlough pay of his rank.

VI. Upon certificate of a Medical Board, an officer not entitled to furlough, if he has served not less than two years in India, may receive it for any period not exceeding two years; but such furlough will be deducted from his next accruing furlough until it is paid off. An officer will not be allowed to anticipate furlough under this rule to an aggregate amount of more than two years.

NOTE (1).—The grant of furlough on medical certificate is restricted to a maximum period of one year in the first instance.

NOTE (2).—An officer of less than two years' service from date of coming under the new rules, falling sick, will be sent to England on medical certificate, on English furlough pay, without the period of his furlough being fixed, subject to the decision of His Majesty's Government as to his ultimate disposal; a special report on each case being submitted to His Majesty's Government by the Government of India, or in the case of officers of the Madras or Bombay Army by the Government of those Presidencies. (See Notes 2 and 3 to Schedule A.)

NOTE (3).—Furlough to the extent of two years in all may be anticipated a second time under Rule VI when the first anticipation was rendered necessary by wounds or illness contracted by active service in the field.

VII. An officer who has exhausted the furlough obtainable under these rules, and who may be certified by a Medical Board to be still unfit for duty, will be placed temporarily or permanently on half pay, according to the circumstances of the case.

VIII. Under very urgent circumstances, special furlough may be granted for private affairs, which need not be paid back; but it will be in all cases without any kind of pay, and will not count for pension.

NOTE.—The amount of leave which may be granted under this rule will be restricted to such period as the Government granting it is satisfied is actually necessary with reference to the urgency of the case.

IX. Officers and Warrant Officers proceeding on furlough to Europe may, if they desire it, receive an advance equivalent to three months' furlough pay.

X. Leave may be granted for any period not exceeding six months at a time, and may not except for very urgent reasons, be specially reported to the Government of India, be renewed till after six months have elapsed from its expiration. It must be spent at some place east of the 40th degree of east longitude. Subject to these conditions, it may be granted or refused entirely at the discretion of the local authorities, who in granting it shall fix its duration, and may fix the limits within which it shall be spent, and whether during its continuance half pay and allowances are to be deducted for the payment of a substitute; and they may grant it as preparatory to furlough, or for the purpose of a medical examination. Leave counts as service for pension and furlough. The local authorities shall be defined for the purpose of this rule by the Government of India.

XI. Leave and furlough are reckoned from and to the close of the days named in the order granting them.

NOTE.—From and after the 17th November, 1883, the day on which the vessel on which an officer proceeds on furlough quits her moorings or anchorage, whether she leaves the limits of the port or not on that day, will be reckoned as the date of the commencement of his furlough, and as a day of the furlough; and the date on which the vessel on which he returns to India drops her anchor or is moored at any Indian port, will be reckoned as a day of duty. An officer's furlough begins on the vessel's departure from any Indian port at which he first joins her, and ends on the day before her arrival at the port at which he last quits her.

XII. The position of the officers not included in Schedule A will be unchanged as respects leave or furlough.

XIII. Officers now in the service placing themselves, from a given date, under these rules, shall count service for pension up to that date under the rules to which they were then subject.

NOTE.—An officer who elects the regulations of 1875, takes with him so much service for pension as he had become entitled to under former rules.

When, however, officers joining the Staff Corps have joined with less than 20 years' service under the rules of 1854 or 1868, before coming under these rules, they shall, after completing 20 years' service in all, be permitted to count as service for pension a proportion of any furlough or leave they may have taken under earlier rules, at the rate of two years' leave for 20 years' service, or one-tenth. Thus, an officer who, before coming under the rules of 1875, had a service of 14 years and 99 days, of which two years 142 days were on leave under the rules of 1868, would count one year and 156 days' leave, and would have 13 years 173 days' total service for pension.

INDIAN STAFF CORPS—continued.FURLOUGH RULES OF 1875—continued.SCHEDULE A.

Officers entering the Staff Corps or Indian Medical Service, and Warrant Officers attaining that rank, after the 31st December, 1875; and

Any other officers, Commissioned or Warrant, belonging to the Indian Army or Medical Service, or officers of the Staff Corps, who, after the 31st December, 1875, shall, before asking for leave or furlough, apply in writing to their local Governments to be placed definitely upon this Schedule; but their service for furlough under Rule I shall commence from the date of such entry or such application respectively.

NOTE 1.—The date of entry into the Staff Corps is that for choice of furlough rules, viz., the date on which the officer was originally appointed on probation. An officer, therefore, whose date of admission to the Staff Corps is not after the 31st December, 1875, will have the choice of rules, but an officer whose date of admission is after that date will have no option.

NOTE 2.—Officers compulsorily placed under these regulations on entering the Staff Corps, will be allowed to count their previous military service in India towards furlough, provided they shall have served five years in India, of which not less than half shall have been in the Staff Corps; but any leave out of India on private affairs taken by them during service so counted will be reckoned as furlough taken under Rule I, and any leave taken on Medical certificate will be reckoned as taken under Rule VI.

Should, therefore, an officer, who took sick leave out of India whilst in the British service, again require leave on Medical certificate on his admission to the Staff Corps, and proceed under Rule VI, he will be granted, in the first instance, only so much furlough as will complete the period admissible under that rule. This

furlough may be extended to two years on "English Furlough Pay," but it will rest with His Majesty's Government to determine whether any, and if so, what, extension shall be granted.

NOTE 3.—Departmental Officers with honorary rank or Warrant Officers promoted to warrant grade after the 31st December, 1875, and thus compulsorily brought under these rules, will be allowed to count their previous service for furlough under Rule VI; but furlough is not to be granted under this rule until a Warrant Officer has completed two years' service since his last return to duty from furlough as a Non-Commissioned Officer, otherwise he can only be granted leave under Note 2 to Rule VI.

NOTE 4.—An officer electing these regulations and having furlough to his credit under previous rules, forfeits such furlough absolutely, he being required to serve five years from date of such election to entitle him to one years' furlough, unless it be sick furlough. An officer similarly circumstanced, but having furlough to his debit, is equally with the others entitled to take one year's furlough five years after electing the new rules.

Any officers in civil employment declared by the Government of India to be under Civil Leave rules shall not be under these rules.

Note.—These regulations are not applicable to Commissioned Officers employed in the Public Works Department, but they are applicable to Honorary Commissioned and Warrant Officers so employed.

* * *The last pay certificate issued to an officer to whom furlough or leave has been granted shall state the pay to which, under Rule V, he is at that date entitled in respect to the office he is holding, noticing the date, if any fixed, at which he will vacate it.

SUBSIDIARY RULES.

I. No "extra or special allowances" drawn by an officer, which are now excluded from the account in calculating furlough pay under the rules of 1868, can be reckoned in calculating furlough pay under these rules (Rule V).

II. The Governments of the three Presidencies, or their Excellencies the Commanders-in-Chief, in regard to officers serving directly under them, shall be held to be the "local authorities" named in Rule X.

III. Pending further orders, a Staff Officer granted leave under Rule X shall receive, as heretofore, the pay or Staff Corps pay of his rank, and half the staff salary of his appointment during such leave; but full staff salary may be allowed him for the first two months (as now during Privilege Leave) when he makes approved arrangements for the performance of his duties during that period. In such cases the G. O. granting the leave must state that the first two months are to be on full staff pay.

The leave up to sixty days in each year on full staff pay under the foregoing rule may be taken either for the full time or by instalments,

in the same way as Privilege Leave under other furlough rules is taken, the condition that six months must elapse between the return of an officer to duty and the grant of a fresh period of leave not being enforced in respect of such leave of absence.

NOTE 1.—Privilege Leave for 90 days, after 33 months' duty, which may be spent anywhere in or out of India, is admissible to Commissioned and Warrant Officers under these rules; but to claim the privilege it shall be necessary for an officer to show that he has had no leave on full Indian pay and allowances for 33 months previous to the date of his application, and that he has made satisfactory arrangements for the performance of his duties during his absence, at no additional cost to the State. If leave for a shorter period than 90 days is taken, the balance is forfeited.

NOTE 2.—Officers are not required to report themselves to the India Office should they go to England; but it will be incumbent on them to keep their departmental superiors in India acquainted with their address.

NOTE 3.—No furlough or leave out of India, if

INDIAN STAFF CORPS—continued.FURLOUGH RULES OF 1875—continued.

exceeding six months, can be counted as part of the required 33 months' duty; but an officer who may have 33 months' service at credit before proceeding on such furlough or leave (in excess of six months) may be allowed to take accumulated Privilege Leave on his return, if he can be spared.

NOTE 4.—Warrant Officers proceeding out of India on Privilege Leave under Note 1 must deposit the amount of their return passage money, or give security for the payment of the same.

NOTE 5.—General Officers Commanding Divisions, Districts, and Brigades, are not eligible for the 90 days' Privilege Leave referred to in the above Note 1.

NOTE 6.—The "Staff Officers" referred to in the above Rule III apply to regimental as well as Departmental Staff.

N.B.—It is distinctly to be understood that the rule regarding the pay to be drawn on leave under Rule X is liable to be modified at any time in any way that the Government of India may deem expedient.

SUPPLEMENTARY REGULATIONS FOR MILITARY OFFICERS IN CIVIL EMPLOY:—

I. Any officer now in civil employ, who is subject to the Military Furlough Regulations of 1854 or 1868, shall remain so subject.

II. Any Military Officer who may hereafter

enter civil employ, who is not already subject to the Military Furlough and Leave Rules of 1875, shall remain subject to the Rules applicable to him when he enters civil employ.

NOTE.—An officer referred to in Rules I and II cannot elect the Military Furlough and Leave Rules of 1875, so long as he continues in civil employ.

III. Any officer who may enter civil employ, who is already subject to the Military Furlough and Leave Rules of 1875, shall be subject to the rules in the Civil Leave Code applicable to covenanted civil servants, both as regards furlough and leave taken out of India and in India.

IV. If an officer, who has been subject to the rules in the Civil Service Regulations under the third of these rules, is again employed in the Military Department, and so again becomes subject to the Military Furlough and Leave Rules of 1875, any furlough or special leave taken by him under the Civil Service Regulations will be treated in calculating the furlough admissible to him under the said Military Rules as if it had been furlough taken under the said Military Rules. Privilege Leave taken under the Civil Service Regulations will be treated as if it had been leave taken under the 10th Military Rule. Subsidiary or Examination Leave taken under the Civil Service Regulations will not affect the claims of such an officer under the Military Furlough and Leave Rules.

TEMPORARY HALF PAY.

An officer placed on temporary half pay at the recommendation of the Medical Board of the India Office will be examined by the same Board as to his fitness to return to duty every two years, and if still reported unfit at the third examination, that is, after being six years on the temporary half-pay list, he will be transferred to the retired list on permanent half pay, or on pension if entitled thereto. An officer reported to be unfit at the commencement of the second period, i.e., after two years on half pay, may, however, be permitted voluntarily to retire on permanent half pay or pension.

An officer unable to appear before the Medical Board at the stated times will be required to furnish a certificate, signed by two medical men, as to his inability. An officer who is continuously unable to appear will be retired permanently after six years without further report.

Officers who, in view to being restored to the active list, may wish to appear before the Board

at other times than those specified, may be permitted to do so. Officers ordered periodically to appear before the Board will receive their travelling expenses and lodging allowance on the War Office scale, but those appearing at other times, at their own wish, must do so at their own expense.

If the removal to half pay was in consequence of ill-health contracted in the performance of military duty, time on temporary half pay not exceeding one year is allowed to reckon as service for pension, and, after restoration to full pay, for promotion also; but an officer cannot be promoted while on the half-pay list. Transfer of an officer to temporary half pay involves the vacation of his regimental or other appointment.

An officer on temporary half pay must not proceed beyond the limits of the United Kingdom without the special permission of the India Office.

India Office.
April, 1901.

Retirement.

Officers are subject to compulsory retirement in the grades of Lieutenant-Colonel, Colonel, or General Officer, on account of age. General Officers thus retired will receive—

Yearly.
In any grade, at 62 years of age ... £ 800
Lieutenant-Generals, at 67 years of age ... 900
Generals, at 67 years of age ... 1,000

They may retire voluntarily—

Yearly.
In any grade, at 60 years of age, on Lieutenant-General, at 65 years of age, on ... £ 750
General, at 65 years of age, on ... 850
no General Officer receiving less, on retirement, than £700 a year. 950

INDIAN STAFF CORPS—continued.

RETIREMENT—continued.

Other officers are entitled to pensions at the following rates:—

Yearly.
£
After 20 years' service for pension ... 250
" 24 " " " " ... 365
" 28 " " " " ... 500
" 32 " " " " ... 700

An officer who has become incapacitated for further service in India on account of unfitness caused by duty, may, after he has been two years on the temporary half-pay list, be granted an invalid pension according to the following scale:—

Per annum.
£
After 15 years' pensionable service 150
" 16 " " " " " 170
" 17 " " " " " 190
" 18 " " " " " 210
" 19 " " " " " 230

Service for pension reckons from date of arrival in India with the addition (except in the

case of an officer exchanging into the Staff Corps) of service out of India before joining the Staff Corps to an extent not exceeding two years in any case.

Time on half pay not exceeding one year is allowed to reckon as service for pension in the case of an officer placed on half pay on account of ill-health contracted in the performance of military duty.

After being six years on temporary half pay, officers unfit for restoration to the effective list are transferred to the retired list on permanent half pay unless entitled to pension. After two years on half pay, an officer unfit for duty may voluntarily retire on half pay.

Officers in civil employ struck off the effective list of the army after 10 years' absence from military duty retain their claims to pension under military rules.

A retired officer is permitted, under certain conditions, to commute a portion not exceeding one-third of his pension, which commutation may take place on retirement, or at any time subsequent thereto.

FAMILY PENSIONS.

The claims to pension of the widows and families of officers of the Staff Corps are treated under the provisions of such Royal Warrant regulating the grant of pension to the widows and families of officers of H.M. British Forces, as may be in force for the time being.

The following are the rates of ordinary pension:—

Compassionate allowance
Pension to Widow. Yearly. to each child. Yearly.
£ £
General Officer, including
  Brigadier-General ... 120 20
  Colonel ... 100 16
  Lieutenant-Colonel ... 90 16
  Major ... 70 14
  Captain ... 50 12
  Lieutenant ... 40 10

with increased pensions in the cases of the widows and children of officers who have been killed in action or have died of wounds or illness contracted on field service.

The compassionate allowance of a son ceases at 18 years of age, and that of a daughter at

21 years of age, or at the expiration of the year commencing on the 1st April in which the son is otherwise previously provided for or the daughter is married.

Widows and families are also entitled to pensions under the Indian Military Service Family Pension Regulations, for which all officers must subscribe as a condition of their appointment to the Staff Corps.

The following are the rates of widows' pension:—

Yearly.
£
Lieutenant-Colonel of five years' service in the rank (if subscribed for)* ... 160
Lieutenant-Colonel ... 130
Major ... 100
Captain ... 70
Lieutenant ... 40

Children's pensions (all ranks alike) dependent on age:—

From 10l. to 45l. per annum.

Pensions to sons cease at 21 years of age, to daughters on marriage.

* Subscription for this pension is voluntary.

Instruction in England.

Classes open to Officers of the Indian Staff Corps while on leave in England.

Examinations in England.

1. An officer of the Indian Staff Corps who is desirous of joining a class of instruction while on leave in England must apply in writing to the Under Secretary of State for India, India Office, London, S.W. Applicants will be in-

formed of their appointment to a class shortly before the date fixed for its assembling. Extension of leave will not be granted to enable an officer to undergo instruction or to complete a course.

INDIAN STAFF CORPS—continued,
INSTRUCTION IN ENGLAND—continued.

2. The principal classes of instruction are Veterinary, held at the Army Veterinary School, Aldershot; Military Engineering and Military Sketching, held at the School of Military Engineering, Chatham, and Practical Instruction, held at various stations. [Some of these classes have been temporarily suspended.] Officers should apply to the India Office for information regarding the dates of assembling of each class in 1902.

3. The subjects of the courses of practical instruction are specified in the King's Regulations, 1899, paras. 1163-69. These courses are intended to assist officers in preparing for examination in subjects (c), (d), and (g), prescribed for Captains of the British Army before promotion to Major (King's Regulations, paras. 1172-93 and Appendix VII) which is the equivalent of Part II of the examination prescribed for Lieutenants and Captains of the Staff Corps* in Article 233 of Indian Army Regulations, Vol. II., Sect. V. An officer on leave, who has not passed Part I, can only be admitted to the examination in Part II on the understanding that he will pass Part I within six months of his return to his appointment in India; failing which he will be required to pass Part II again. An officer who passes in Military Law while in England is required to pass a supplementary examination in the Indian Articles of War and Indian Military Law on his return to India.

The examinations at Home Stations begin on the first Monday in May and November.

Applications from officers of the Staff Corps for permission to attend for examination (whether they have previously gone through a course of practical instruction or not) should reach the India Office by 27th March and 27th September, respectively, and should specify, in order of preference, two or three of the stations preferred.

4. The daily rates of lodging allowance, when quarters are not provided, are as follows:—

For a field officer ... ... ... 8s.
„ captain ... ... ... 6s.
„ subaltern ... ... ... 4s.

To enable an officer to receive this allowance, he must send to the India Office, on completing the course, his certificate of qualification (which will be returned), and also a certificate signed by competent authority, showing that he was not provided with quarters, and stating the dates from and to which he was under instruction.

As there is no examination at the conclusion of the "practical courses," the lodging allowance for attending these will only be admissible on passing the next examination in subjects (c), (d), and (g) in the following May or November, as the case may be.

No travelling or other allowances are granted.
5. Any application for permission to go through a course of instruction in gymnastics or fencing and single-stick practice at a military gymnasium, or to attend a riding-class or any class of instruction not specified in this memorandum, will be specially considered, but no lodging allowance will be granted in respect of any such course.

6. On joining a class of instruction an officer must take his uniform with him.

7. Officers of the Indian Staff Corps are not admitted to classes of musketry instruction at Hythe, nor to signalling courses at Aldershot.

8. Special attention is directed to the following G.O.C.C., dated 15th January, 1887:—

"As great inconvenience and unnecessary trouble has been caused by officers of the Indian Army on furlough in England withdrawing from classes of instruction which they have applied for permission to join, and to which, after correspondence between the India Office and the War Office, they have been duly appointed, it is notified that those who have received permission to join schools or classes of instruction in England will not, except under very special circumstances, be allowed to withdraw their names."

(Signed) E. STEDMAN, Major-General,
Military Secretary.

* See page 284.

RECALL OF RETIRED OFFICERS IN TIMES OF EMERGENCY.

VICTORIA R.I.

WHEREAS we deem it expedient that officers who may enter Our Indian Staff Corps on or after the date of this Warrant, and who may retire on pension, shall be liable to be recalled to service in Our Indian Forces in time of emergency:

Our Will and Pleasure is as follows:—

1. An officer who shall have entered the Indian Staff Corps subsequent to the date of this Warrant, and who may retire on pension, shall remain liable to be recalled to service in Our Indian Forces in time of emergency, in accordance with such Regulations as may from time to

time be made by our Secretary of State for India in Council.

2. Officers thus recalled to service shall, during their employment on army service, exercise the authority of officers of similar rank in Our Regular Forces with respect to the troops composing Our Indian Forces, or any other part of Our Regular Forces, and shall take precedence in their rank according to the date of their former commissions.

Given at Our Court at Osborne, this ninth day of January, one thousand eight hundred and ninety-two, in the Fifty-fifth year of Our Reign.

By Her Majesty's command,
(Signed) CROSS.

For Particulars regarding Admission to the Indian Staff Corps see
"Appointments to the Indian Services."
